Document [0] (Title: Dupilumab -- Contraindications -- Acute bronchospasm, asthma, corticosteroid withdrawal, and status asthmaticus) While dupilumab is approved as an adjuvant or maintenance therapy to treat eosinophilic and glucocorticoid-dependent moderate-to-severe asthma, it should not be used by itself to treat episodes of asthma exacerbations or acute bronchospasm. [31] Patients with comorbid asthma who start dupilumab therapy for atopic dermatitis or chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis should be advised to stay on their asthma medications to prevent the onset of any adverse event. [31] However, a study by Rabe et al revealed that for patients on oral glucocorticoid therapy for moderate-to-severe asthma or acute asthma exacerbations, adding dupilumab not only led to an overall reduction in the glucocorticoid use but also alleviation of symptoms associated with acute asthmatic episodes or exacerbations over 24 weeks. The only notable but transient adverse effect was reversible eosinophilia in about 14% of the patients. [35]
Document [1] (Title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications) Dupilumab has been shown to enhance lung function and decrease severe exacerbations in patients with uncontrolled persistent asthma, irrespective of baseline eosinophil count, and when used in addition to inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) and long-acting β-2 agonists (LABA), as compared to standard asthma therapy. [6] [7] The American Thoracic Society (ATS) and European Respiratory Society (ERS) guidelines recommend using dupilumab as an additional treatment option for patients with severe eosinophilic asthma or dependence on oral corticosteroids. [8]

Document [6] (Title: Dupilumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Dupilumab is an effective and safe immunomodulating therapy for treating moderate-to-severe symptoms associated with atopic dermatitis, including itching, poor sleep quality, anxiety, and depression. Evidence from increasing trials suggests that the benefits of dupilumab far outweigh its side effects. Since no concrete literature-based evidence suggests that dupilumab is contraindicated during pregnancy or lactation, shared decision-making among healthcare team members and patients is paramount for ensuring safe and effective treatment plans. Patients should receive significant education on their treatment options, including discussing the benefits and risks to maternal and fetal health, and that patient values and preferences are considered. Consultation with an immunologist is required before initiating dupilumab for severe asthma. Moderate to severe atopic dermatitis is best managed in consultation with a dermatologist. Otorhinolaryngologist consultation is necessary for patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis. Both pharmacists and nurses need easy and direct access to the prescriber so any patient concerns regarding dupilumab use can be addressed immediately. Communication among healthcare team members is pivotal to obtaining optimal therapeutic results for dupilumab use with minimal adverse events.

Document [13] (Title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- Asthma Phenotype) Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) guidelines show that population-level recommendations depend on type II or non-type II phenotypes. Type II inflammation is found in patients with severe asthma and atopy. It is associated with allergic conditions such as nasal polyposis and atopic dermatitis. Elevated serum, sputum eosinophils, and increased FeNO characterize type 2 inflammation. Management includes optimal use of ICS and targeted biologics. Dupilumab is indicated in patients with severe eosinophilia or type 2 asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS-LABA. Pediatric patients with persistently elevated IgE levels respond well to omalizumab. [49] [7]
Document [14] (Title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Biological Agents) Biological agents are possible for children aged 6 and older who have not responded to traditional therapies. The candidates for omalizumab, a monoclonal antibody against IgE, are children with sensitization to at least 1 perennial aeroallergen and moderate-to-severe asthma. Dupilumab, another monoclonal antibody targeting the IL-4 receptor and inhibiting IL-4 and IL-13 signaling, is used in children aged 6 and older as additional maintenance therapy. Mepolizumab, which targets the IL-5 receptor, is utilized in children with severe eosinophilic asthma.
Document [15] (Title: Dupilumab -- Continuing Education Activity) Dupilumab is an injectable prescription medication administered subcutaneously to treat severe and refractory forms of atopic dermatitis. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the medication in April 2017 to treat moderate-to-severe, resistant, atopic dermatitis unresponsive to conventional therapy, including topical corticosteroids. Dupilumab is also used as a maintenance treatment for asthma and chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis in adults and children, in conjunction with other medications.
Document [16] (Title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications) Dupilumab is a human monoclonal antibody administered subcutaneously to inhibit the interleukin-4 receptor subunit α (IL-4R α). The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved dupilumab in April 2017 for the treatment of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis (AD) in adults and pediatric patients aged 6 and older, as well as for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis (CRSwNP) in adults. [1] Since the incidence of atopic dermatitis has been rising, especially in the developed and industrialized parts of the world, dermatologists must prescribe treatment regimens that are both effective and safe to use in the long-term management of this chronic skin condition. [2] Dupilumab is also used in treating adults with atopic dermatitis symptoms inadequately controlled with topical corticosteroid use. [3] [4] Currently, no specific guidelines deem the drug safe for children younger than 6. [4] [5]

Document [26] (Title: Allergic and Environmentally Induced Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Step-Up Therapy: The Goal of Asthma Control) Step 1: For intermittent asthma, preferred therapy is a short-acting inhaled beta2 agonist. For persistent asthma, daily medication is recommended. Step 2: Preferred treatment is a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid. Step 3: A low dose inhaled corticosteroid plus a long-acting inhaled beta2 agonist is recommended, or a medium-dose inhaled corticosteroid. Step 4: The preferred treatment is a medium-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus a long-acting beta2 agonist. Step 5: The preferred treatment is high dose inhaled corticosteroid plus a long-acting beta2 agonist and considering omalizumab for people with allergies. Step 6: The preferred treatment is high-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus a long-acting beta2 agonist plus an oral corticosteroid; consider omalizumab with people with allergies.

Document [28] (Title: Dupilumab -- Mechanism of Action) Dupilumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody of the immunoglobulin G4 subclass designed to inhibit receptor signaling downstream of the JAK-STAT pathway by blocking interleukin-4/interleukin-13 (IL-4/IL-13) receptors. [4] As an IL-4Rα antagonist, dupilumab inhibits the signaling of pro-inflammatory cytokines, or interleukins, that induce inflammatory and immunological responses in several conditions, including eczema, asthma, allergic reactions, and rhinosinusitis. [18] Essentially, IL-4Rα is a component common to both IL-4 and IL-13 receptor complexes and is ubiquitously expressed in innate and adaptive immune cells to promote the signaling of IL-4 and IL-13. [19] [20] By blocking this pathway, dupilumab affects 3 integral disease mechanisms of atopic dermatitis: the decrease of skin barrier function, the class switch to IgE, and the Th2 differentiation. [4] [21]

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400) Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690) Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 60) Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases”, indicating that they are systemic therapies used in conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Metaproterenol is listed both as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist for systemic use and as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalant, reflecting its availability for systemic administration as well as inhalation. The hierarchy places non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonists for systemic use under the broader class “adrenergics for systemic use”, while non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants belong to the class “adrenergics, inhalants”. Alpha‑ and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants are also a subclass of “adrenergics, inhalants”. The overarching category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES” is linked inversely to both “adrenergics, inhalants” and “adrenergics for systemic use”, and it is a subclass of “OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES, INHALANTS in ATC”. This classification scheme illustrates how systemic biologic agents, inhaled adrenergic drugs, and broader therapeutic groups are organized to reflect their pharmacologic actions and roles in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

Antihistamines for systemic use are expressly excluded from the “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” category (inverse‑ISA), and the respiratory‑substance category is not a subclass of “other respiratory system products in ATC”. Collectively, the knowledge base delineates a network of drug classes used in obstructive airway disease management: monoclonal antibodies (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab) for severe inflammatory phenotypes; xanthine‑based bronchodilators (bamifylline, aminofylin, dyfylin, fenspiride, Guaithylline) that also intersect with psychostimulant/ADHD therapies; bronchodilatancia as a methacholin‑derived bronchodilator; and phenothiazine antihistamines for allergic inflammation. Understanding these hierarchical, classificatory and contraindication relationships supports comprehensive patient care by guiding clinicians in selecting systemic therapies that address both respiratory pathology and ancillary pharmacologic effects.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 745) Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in ATC,” indicating that they are classified as systemic agents used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. Benralizumab is explicitly listed as a member of this group, and mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are also linked to it through the same membership relationship. In addition, amlexanox, benralizumab, bromodiphenhydramine, fenoterol, kromoglykát dvojsodný, methapyrilen, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab share the therapeutic classification “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances affecting the respiratory system). This classification is expressed by multiple “has_therapeutic_class” relationships, showing that each of these agents is considered a respiratory‑system‑active drug. The inverse‑ISA relationship places “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” as a subclass of the broader ATC category “other respiratory system products in ATC,” situating these drugs within the larger family of respiratory therapeutics. Together, these relationships illustrate that the listed biologics (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab) and small‑molecule agents (amlexanox, fenoterol, bromodiphenhydramine, kromoglykát dvojsodný, methapyrilen) are unified by their role as systemic treatments for obstructive airway diseases and by their inclusion in the therapeutic class of respiratory‑system‑affecting substances. Understanding this hierarchical and categorical organization helps clinicians navigate the ATC system, select appropriate therapies, and appreciate the diverse mechanisms—ranging from anti‑eosinophilic biologics to bronchodilators and mast‑cell stabilizers—available for managing respiratory disorders.

Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 700) The class “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” comprises several specific inhaled combination products that pair a β‑adrenergic bronchodilator with an inhaled corticosteroid to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory therapy for obstructive airway diseases. Included in this class are albuterol + beclomethasone drug combinations, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone, and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate. Each of these formulations is a member of the adrenergic‑corticosteroid combination category, distinguishing them from inhaled therapies that contain anticholinergic agents. This grouping highlights their shared pharmacologic strategy of combining a rapid‑acting or long‑acting β‑agonist with a corticosteroid, which is central to modern asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) management.

Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1209) Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics is a therapeutic class that comprises several fixed‑dose inhaler formulations used to treat obstructive airway diseases. Within this class, the albuterol + beclomethasone drug combination, the budesonide / formoterol combination, and the formoterol / mometasone combination are all members. Each of these products pairs a rapid‑acting β2‑adrenergic agonist (albuterol or formoterol) with an inhaled corticosteroid (beclomethasone, budesonide, or mometasone) to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory effects while deliberately excluding anticholinergic agents. This grouping highlights the shared pharmacologic strategy of combining adrenergic bronchodilators with corticosteroids to improve symptom control and reduce exacerbations in patients with asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Non‑steroidal antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases are also “isa” members of the same ATC inhalant group, highlighting their role as steroid‑free anti‑allergic agents. The overarching category DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES is an inverse‑isa of both adrenergics, inhalants and other inhaled drug classes, thereby encompassing all inhaled adrenergic agents, inhaled corticosteroids, antiallergics, and related combination products, while being inversely related to adrenergics for systemic use to distinguish inhaled from systemic formulations. These hierarchical and membership relationships provide a comprehensive framework for selecting appropriate bronchodilator, anti‑inflammatory, and combination therapies based on route of administration, receptor selectivity, and contraindications, supporting safe and effective patient care in asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and related obstructive airway conditions.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 55) Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Fenspiride is additionally listed as a member of the ATC subgroup “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants,” indicating that it is recognized in both systemic and inhalant categories for the treatment of obstructive airway conditions. The class “Anti‑inflammatories, Inhaled Corticosteroids” is linked to the inhalant subgroup “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants” and comprises specific corticosteroid agents—beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát, and flutikason propionát—as child entities. The broader category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES” sits above both the inhalant and systemic subgroups, as shown by its inverse‑ISA relationship to each, thereby serving as an overarching classification that encompasses all systemic and inhalant therapies for obstructive airway diseases. This hierarchical organization clarifies how individual drugs and drug families are grouped within the ATC system, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic agents for managing obstructive airway disorders.
